title: Hormuz Half-Open, Oil Half-Cheer: The Reopening That Isn't Yet
subhead: The Strait of Hormuz is inching back to life under a fragile US-Iran deal, but mines, transit fees, and insurance gaps mean full recovery is months away. Meanwhile, OPEC+ hikes quotas it can't fill, Putin concedes fuel shortages, and Turkey's expiring Iran gas contract tests the new sanctions architecture.

sections.heading: Hormuz: Reopening in Name, Not Yet in Practice
sections.body:
The US-Iran preliminary agreement to end hostilities and reopen the Strait of Hormuz has shifted market sentiment, but the physical reality on the water tells a more complicated story. According to Kpler data cited by Reuters and MSN, tanker traffic through Hormuz has increased from a May low of 9.6 million bpd to around 12 million bpd — still well below the ~21 million bpd that transited before the war. Mines must be cleared from shipping lanes, war-risk insurance premiums remain elevated, and Iran's IRGC attacked a Singapore-flagged cargo vessel near the strait on June 26, signalling that the waterway is far from secure.

Rystad Energy chief economist Claudio Galimberti told Al-Monitor: 'Sentiment is not the same as supply.' Retaliatory Iranian strikes on Gulf state energy infrastructure took pumps and refineries offline, and restarting those is not simply turning a tap. Francis Perrin of IRIS noted that for refined products and LNG, if Hormuz is blocked, you're blocked 100 percent — unlike crude, which can partially bypass via the UAE's new Fujairah pipeline.

The IEA's June oil market report warned that a full recovery may not come for months, noting that 'mines will have to be removed from the main shipping lanes and supply chains will take time to normalize.' Iran has also floated maritime 'service fees' on transiting tankers — a toll that companies will likely pass on to consumers.
sections.whyItMatters: The gap between the deal announcement and operational reality is where the risk lives. Prices fell to ~$78/bbl on deal optimism, but global oil inventories have shed ~3.8 million bpd since the conflict began, and the IEA warns stocks could hit historic lows before any surplus emerges.

sections.heading: OPEC+ Hikes Quotas It Cannot Fill
sections.body:
OPEC+ approved its fourth consecutive monthly output increase on June 7, adding 188,000 bpd from July — the same size hike as June. The seven core members (Saudi Arabia, Iraq, Kuwait, Algeria, Kazakhstan, Russia, and Oman) have now raised quotas by nearly 600,000 bpd since April. But actual production tells a different story: the group's output collapsed to 33.19 million bpd in April versus 42.77 million in February, per OPEC's own figures, because Gulf members cannot export through Hormuz.

Rystad analyst Jorge Leon, a former OPEC official, told CNA: 'An OPEC+ production increase means very little while the Strait of Hormuz remains closed.' He warned that 'when Hormuz reopens, the market could move very quickly from fear of shortage to fear of surplus.' The UAE's exit from OPEC in May, ending almost 60 years of membership, further undermines the cartel's ability to coordinate output — though the UAE is still increasing its own production outside the framework.

OPEC+ also affirmed it is reviewing members' production capacity assessments to set 2027 baselines, and the current 2023-era 1.65 million bpd cut is on track to be fully unwound by September if monthly hikes of ~188,000 bpd continue.
sections.whyItMatters: OPEC+ is performing output discipline while physically unable to deliver. The real test comes when Hormuz reopens and Gulf producers flood the market — IEA projects a ~8 million bpd supply surge in 2027, heavily outpacing demand recovery.

sections.heading: Putin Concedes Fuel Shortages as Ukrainian Drone Campaign Bites
sections.body:
Vladimir Putin acknowledged for the first time on June 28 that Russia faces a 'certain deficit' of fuel, following months of Ukrainian drone strikes on refineries and fuel depots. According to Ukrainska Pravda, Putin told a meeting on fuel supplies that authorities had temporarily banned petrol and aviation fuel exports and were considering a complete ban on diesel exports. Reuters estimates Russia has lost around 25% of its petrol production capacity, while overall refining volumes have fallen to a 21-year low.

The damage is spreading geographically. Reuters reported that fuel shortages have spread from annexed Crimea to parts of southern Russia and even to Moscow. In Crimea, occupation authorities restricted petrol sales to government representatives only. Russian newspaper Kommersant reported that refineries are now allowed to sell gasoline with sulphur levels 15 times the European maximum. Tatneft's major refinery in Udmurtia shut down completely after drone strikes.

Putin claimed the damage was 'not critical' and said Russia would increase air defence production and speed up repairs. But the Kremlin's own emergency measures — export bans, substandard fuel waivers, possible fuel imports — tell a different story.
sections.whyItMatters: Ukraine's drone campaign has moved from symbolic strikes to a genuine logistics crisis inside Russia. Export bans redirect domestic shortfall away from global markets, but the bigger signal is that Russia's energy infrastructure — long assumed resilient — has proven brittle under sustained assault.

sections.heading: Turkey-Iran Gas: The Molecule Flows, the Payment Doesn't
sections.body:
Turkey's 25-year natural gas supply contract with Iran expires this July, and the constraint on any renewal is no longer physical supply but payment. A detailed Forbes analysis by Gueney Yildiz explains that three converging developments create a sanctions wall: the JCPOA snapback restoring UN sanctions in September 2025, Halkbank's June 2026 deferred prosecution agreement barring Iran-benefiting transactions, and an OFAC general license covering Iranian oil and petrochemicals but pointedly excluding natural gas.

Turkey has diversified significantly — 10-year LNG deals with ExxonMobil and Shell, growing Black Sea Sakarya field output — and Iranian gas is now just ~13% of imports (7.8 bcm in 2025), down from a position where it was irreplaceable. But Forbes notes that eastern Anatolia's pipeline infrastructure was built for east-to-west flows, and reverse-flow LNG substitution during cold snaps is not yet viable. New LNG cargoes from the major contracts don't start arriving until 2027.

The OFAC gas exclusion is not an oversight. It is a deliberate signal. As Forbes writes, the Turkey-Iran gas expiry is 'the first live test of whether that line actually reaches a low-profile, mutually convenient energy link, or whether pragmatism reopens the same exception it always has.'
sections.whyItMatters: This is the first real-world test of whether the 2026 sanctions architecture can close previously tolerated energy 'gray zones.' If Turkish banks refuse to process payments to the National Iranian Gas Company, the 25-year corridor closes — not because the gas isn't there, but because the money can't move.

sections.heading: Shadow Fleet Escalation: UK Boards Tanker, France Seizes Vessel
sections.body:
British Royal Marine commandos rappelled from helicopters onto the sanctioned tanker Smyrtos in the English Channel on June 29, in what the UK Defence Ministry called 'the first UK-led operation of its kind.' The Cameroon-flagged vessel had left Russia's Baltic port of Ust-Luga on June 5, bound for Port Said, Egypt. Prime Minister Keir Starmer called it a blow against those 'fueling Putin's war in Ukraine.'

France has seized at least five Russian shadow fleet vessels, according to Forbes, but prosecution efforts have been hindered by gaps in international law. The Forbes analysis by Jill Goldenziel details how shadow fleet vessels exploit flags of convenience, AIS blackouts, and the 1884 submarine cables convention to operate with impunity. The Eagle S — a Cook Islands-flagged ship that cut an electricity cable between Finland and Estonia on Christmas Day 2024 — saw a Finnish court hold the boarding was improper because the cable cutting occurred outside territorial waters.

At the G7 summit in France, Starmer vowed further sanctions targeting Russia's finance networks and shadow fleet, and pledged hundreds of millions of pounds in energy support for Ukraine, including enriched uranium for its nuclear plants.
sections.whyItMatters: The shadow fleet is no longer a sanctions-evasion sideshow — it is an active maritime threat involving cable sabotage, environmental risk, and legal impunity. The UK boarding signals a shift from passive interdiction to kinetic enforcement, but the legal framework remains inadequate.

sections.heading: LNG and Pipeline Infrastructure: Diversification Accelerates
sections.body:
Shell's LNG Outlook 2026, released June 30, projects global LNG demand reaching nearly 700 million tons by 2050, a 65% increase from 2025. But the near term is turbulent: Hormuz disruptions have shut in ~one-fifth of monthly global LNG supply, pushing Asian spot prices above $20/MMBtu at peak. Shell president of integrated gas Cederic Cremers called the conflict 'a system-wide shock' but said the LNG industry 'proved resilient.' Average LNG prices in May were $11-12/MMBtu versus $7-11 in January.

On the infrastructure side, Sempra's Port Arthur Louisiana Connector pipeline entered service on June 9, completing a critical link in the US Gulf Coast LNG export corridor ahead of schedule and under budget. In Europe, the REPowerEU regulation (EU/261/2026) formalizes a phase-out of Russian gas and LNG by late 2027, with remaining Russian imports now down to ~12% of EU gas supply from 45% pre-2022. Pipeline Journal reports that TAP expansion has delivered over 50 bcm cumulatively by 2025, with a first increment of +1.2 bcm/year now online, targeting 20 bcm/year from Azerbaijan by 2027.

Meanwhile, Turkey's 25-year Iran gas contract expiry and the Pakistan-Iran pipeline dispute reaching international arbitration (Pipeline Journal) show that legacy pipeline dependencies are being renegotiated or litigated under new sanctions pressure.
sections.whyItMatters: The Hormuz crisis has not killed LNG investment — it has accelerated it. 180 million tons of new supply capacity is forecast by 2030. But infrastructure bottlenecks in South and Southeast Asia mean the gas may be produced but not yet deliverable, keeping prices volatile through at least 2027.

bottomLine: The US-Iran deal has shifted oil prices downward and sentiment upward, but the physical recovery of Hormuz is a months-long project beset by mines, insurance gaps, and Iran's own IRGC still firing on cargo ships. OPEC+ is hiking quotas it cannot currently fill, setting up a potential glut when Gulf output returns. Ukraine's drone campaign has pushed Putin to acknowledge domestic fuel shortages and ban petrol exports — a remarkable admission from a petrostate. Turkey's expiring Iran gas contract is the first live test of whether the 2026 sanctions architecture can actually close gray-zone energy corridors, and the UK's boarding of the Smyrtos marks a new phase of kinetic enforcement against the Russian shadow fleet. The through-line: energy markets are in a transitional gap between crisis and recovery, and the institutions — OPEC, sanctions regimes, pipeline contracts — that emerge on the other side will look different from what came before.
sources: Al-Monitor, Channel News Asia/Reuters, Forbes (Yildiz), Forbes (Goldenziel), AP, Seeking Alpha, Ukrainska Pravda, Yahoo News, Shell LNG Outlook 2026, Pipeline Journal, Hydrocarbon Processing, CNBC, IEA Monthly Oil Report, Sempra Infrastructure, The Guardian, NPR, UPI
